name: Tennessee
tagline: Home to the world's largest AI supercomputer build (xAI's Memphis 'Colossus'), a brand-new statewide AI Action Plan, and a centralized IT shop in execution mode — Tennessee is one of the country's hottest AI-infrastructure markets.

execSummary.summary: Tennessee enters FY2026 with a $59.5B all-funds budget and an unusually strong AI-infrastructure story for an SLG seller. First, the state is now a national AI-compute epicenter: xAI's 'Colossus' in Memphis is one of the largest AI supercomputers on earth (scaling toward 2GW and ~555,000 GPUs as of January 2026), and Oak Ridge National Laboratory is standing up the 'Lux' AI cluster (early 2026) ahead of its next-gen 'Discovery' exascale system. Second, state government itself is moving fast on AI: a State AI Advisory Council released Tennessee's first statewide AI Action Plan in November 2025, and Strategic Technology Solutions (STS) has already deployed ChatGPT Enterprise to thousands of employees. Third, IT is centralized and in 'execution' mode under new State CIO Kristin Darby, including an ERP modernization that explicitly demands 'native AI architecture.' For Dell, the buying motion runs through the Central Procurement Office's statewide contracts, and the data-and-compute-readiness pillar of the AI plan is a direct opening for governed, secure infrastructure.
opportunities: AI-infrastructure / 'AI factory' demand across the Memphis (xAI Colossus), Nashville, and Oak Ridge corridors (PowerEdge, networking, services).
State AI Action Plan's 'Data & Compute Readiness' pillar — secure statewide infrastructure for safe AI experimentation, a fit for governed on-prem / private-cloud AI.
ERP modernization requiring 'native AI architecture,' plus broad enterprise modernization and automation under STS.
Endpoint, client, and lifecycle refresh across a ~1,200-person central IT org and the agencies it serves.

budget.intro: Tennessee budgets annually on a July 1–June 30 fiscal year. Governor Lee's FY2026 recommended budget totals roughly $59.5B from all revenue sources (about $29.5B in state dollars). There is no single consolidated 'state IT budget' line — technology spend is distributed across agencies and the centralized Strategic Technology Solutions (STS) organization within Finance & Administration, supplemented by large federal flows such as BEAD broadband funding.
notes: Tennessee runs an annual (not biennial) budget; figures above are FY2026 (July 2025–June 2026).
There is no published consolidated state IT line item — confirm agency-level technology budgets and the STS operating budget when forecasting.
AI-infrastructure investment in-state is overwhelmingly private (xAI) and federal (Oak Ridge/DOE), not state-appropriated — the state-government opportunity is modernization and governed AI adoption.

FY2027 budget signed by Gov. Lee May 22, 2026 (~$58B total, ~$29.8B from state dollars); FY begins July 1, 2026. Disciplined posture with a $20.0M rainy-day deposit bringing reserves to $2.2B. Notably includes $50M for AI infrastructure — moving state AI from proof-of-concept toward enterprise-wide use, including an 'AI data management layer' to unify siloed state data — plus non-recurring funds for quantum computing infrastructure.

initiatives.name: xAI 'Colossus' AI Supercomputer — Memphis
initiatives.summary: xAI's Memphis 'Colossus' is among the largest AI training clusters in the world, reported to be scaling toward roughly 2 gigawatts and around 555,000 GPUs as of January 2026, with a stated ambition of one million GPUs. The buildout is anchoring a fast-growing West Tennessee AI-infrastructure ecosystem (power, cooling, networking, supply chain) and reshaping the state's identity as a national compute hub — the single strongest AI-infrastructure tailwind in the market.

initiatives.name: Tennessee Statewide AI Action Plan
initiatives.summary: The State AI Advisory Council — co-chaired by CIO Kristin Darby and F&A Commissioner Jim Bryson — unanimously adopted Tennessee's first statewide AI Action Plan in November 2025. Its four pillars are Modernization & Pilots, Data & Compute Readiness, Workforce & Change Enablement, and Safety/Security/Accountability. The 'Data & Compute Readiness' pillar (secure statewide infrastructure for safe AI experimentation) is a direct opening for governed, auditable on-prem and private-cloud AI.

initiatives.name: STS Enterprise Modernization (ERP + Automation)
initiatives.summary: Under CIO Kristin Darby, STS describes 2026 as a shift into 'execution and action.' A flagship effort is modernizing the state's enterprise resource planning (ERP) system; the RFI explicitly calls for 'native AI architecture' that is extensible, secure, and auditable. Alongside ERP, STS is centered on broad modernization and automation across a ~1,200-person IT organization — a multi-year infrastructure and services runway.

initiatives.name: ChatGPT Enterprise Rollout
initiatives.summary: After a 60-day pilot in 2025, STS moved ChatGPT Enterprise into day-to-day operations, now with 5,000+ state employees licensed (users reportedly saving 1.5–2.5 hours/day). STS built an AI governance framework and steering committee requiring measurable outcomes. The pace signals agency leadership genuinely operationalizing AI — useful context for positioning the secure compute and data foundations that production AI ultimately needs.

initiatives.name: Oak Ridge AI / Exascale Compute
initiatives.summary: Oak Ridge National Laboratory hosts Frontier (the first exascale supercomputer) and is standing up the 'Lux' AI cluster in early 2026, with the next-gen 'Discovery' exascale system slated for 2028. While DOE-funded and procured federally, ORNL anchors East Tennessee as an HPC/AI research hub that pulls in talent, partners, and adjacent research-computing demand.

procurement.intro: Tennessee centralizes most non-IT and technology purchasing through the Central Procurement Office (CPO), a division of the Department of General Services created under TCA § 4-56-104. The CPO issues statewide contracts that state agencies — and, for many categories, local governments and nonprofits — can buy from without running their own solicitation. Suppliers must be registered in the state's Edison system to do business. Strategic Technology Solutions (STS) sets statewide IT direction, standards, and security policy within Finance & Administration.

vehicles.name: CPO Statewide Contracts
vehicles.detail: Primary vehicle. Pre-competed agreements (including a dedicated technology category) usable by state agencies and, for many categories, local government and nonprofits. Confirm Dell agreements and reseller scope are current.

vehicles.name: Edison Supplier Portal
vehicles.detail: Mandatory supplier registration and the transactional backbone for doing business with the state — ensure Dell and partners are correctly registered and represented.

vehicles.name: Cooperative / NASPO ValuePoint
vehicles.detail: Tennessee participates in cooperative purchasing; multi-state cooperative contracts can be a path for technology categories — verify lead-state and TN adoption status.

entities.name: Strategic Technology Solutions (STS)
entities.role: Centralized state IT organization within F&A; sets IT direction, standards, and security policy. ~1,200 staff under CIO Kristin Darby.

entities.name: Central Procurement Office (CPO)
entities.role: Statewide contracts and procurement authority within General Services; the contractual front door for most buys.

entities.name: Department of Finance & Administration (F&A)
entities.role: Parent of STS and the AI Advisory Council; owns enterprise modernization (e.g., ERP).

entities.name: TennCare / large agencies
entities.role: Data-heavy agencies among the larger technology and modernization spenders.

procurement.competition: Tennessee is a centralized, contract-driven market where incumbents hold statewide and cooperative agreements across compute, client, and services. Differentiation comes less from price and more from AI-infrastructure capability, governed/auditable AI offerings aligned to the state's AI Action Plan, and services that map to STS modernization and the Memphis/Oak Ridge compute ecosystem.

powerStructure.name: Bill Lee
powerStructure.title: Governor
powerStructure.note: Champions broadband expansion and the state's AI-leadership and economic-development agenda; owns the FY2026 budget.

powerStructure.name: Kristin Darby
powerStructure.title: State CIO
powerStructure.note: Took office March 31, 2025; ex-healthcare/finance CIO. Leads ~1,200-person STS, co-chairs the AI Advisory Council and State Cybersecurity Council, frames 2026 as 'execution.' Confirmed current — testified to Congress on state cyber funding May 21, 2026.

powerStructure.name: Jim Bryson
powerStructure.title: Commissioner
powerStructure.note: F&A Commissioner and co-chair of the State AI Advisory Council; STS reports up through F&A.

powerStructure.name: AI Advisory Council
powerStructure.title: Statewide AI governance body
powerStructure.note: Authored the November 2025 AI Action Plan; sets the framework for state AI adoption and data/compute readiness.

powerStructure.name: Central Procurement Office
powerStructure.title: Statewide procurement authority
powerStructure.note: Owns the statewide contracts and Edison registration that gate most technology buys.

news.title: State CIO Kristin Darby testifies to Congress on cyber funding, urges SLCGP renewal
news.impact: On May 21, Darby testified before the U.S. House Homeland Security cybersecurity subcommittee that ~$21M in State and Local Cybersecurity Grant Program funds have secured ~90,000 endpoints and trained 21,000+ local-government employees across Tennessee, and pressed Congress to renew the program. Signals a whole-of-state endpoint-security and managed-detection priority — a fit for Dell endpoint, lifecycle, and secure-infrastructure positioning into state and local agencies.

news.title: Gov. Lee signs data-center utility-cost law (HB1847); AI data-center pushback grows
news.impact: Signed May 7 and effective ~June 1, the law requires data-center operators to bear their own electrical/infrastructure costs (and allows self-generation), shielding ratepayers as AI buildout accelerates. In parallel, local moratorium efforts (a ~150,000-signature Nashville-zoo petition; a McMinnville moratorium vote) reflect rising community resistance — shaping where and how 'AI factory' compute lands in-state.

news.title: Gov. Lee signs ~$58B FY2027 budget with $50M for AI infrastructure
news.impact: Enacts a dedicated $50M AI-infrastructure investment — moving state AI from proof-of-concept to enterprise-wide, including an 'AI data management layer' to unify siloed state data. A direct opening for governed compute, data platforms, and secure infrastructure. FY begins July 1, 2026.

news.title: Tennessee secures additional $218M federal broadband grant
news.impact: Adds to BEAD funding to reach remaining unserved Tennesseans; sustained connectivity buildout statewide.

news.title: NTIA approves Tennessee's BEAD final proposal (~$813M)
news.impact: Unlocks a major federal broadband program; expanded last-mile and digital-opportunity activity.

news.title: xAI's Memphis 'Colossus' scales toward ~2GW / ~555,000 GPUs
news.impact: Cements Tennessee as a top-tier national AI-compute hub; massive infrastructure ecosystem demand.

dellActions: Lead with AI infrastructure: connect Dell ISG / AI Factory offerings to the Memphis (xAI), Nashville, and Oak Ridge compute corridors and to the AI Action Plan's 'Data & Compute Readiness' pillar.
Engage STS as a centralized account in 'execution' mode — map Dell modernization, automation, and the ERP 'native AI architecture' requirement to active STS priorities.
Position governed, secure, auditable on-prem / private-cloud AI for sensitive state workloads, aligned to the AI Action Plan's safety and accountability pillar.
Confirm Dell's CPO statewide-contract coverage, Edison registration, and reseller scope are current for the agencies you're targeting.
Refresh endpoint, client, and lifecycle across the ~1,200-person STS org and the agencies it serves; tie to broadband-enabled digital-opportunity programs where relevant.
